source.title: Thalamic control of sensory selection in divided attention

confidence_rationale: Nature paper with causal optogenetic evidence showing TRN subnetworks mediate modality-specific gating of thalamocortical pathways under PFC control. Directly supports ARC-023's TRN-as-routing-switch claim. Confidence limited by mouse-to-human transfer and the fact that modality-specific gating (sensory selection) is not identical to frequency-specific loop gating.
failure_signatures: The study demonstrates modality-specific TRN gating (visual vs auditory), not frequency-specific gating between processing levels (sensory vs motor vs cognitive). The mapping from modality-routing to loop-routing is an inference step.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Prefrontal cortex biases modality-specific TRN subnetworks to control thalamic sensory gain, enabling selective attention across sensory modalities via feedforward inhibition.
mapping.ree_translation: The TRN's modality-specific subnetworks and PFC-dependent gating correspond to ARC-023's claim that TRN acts as an inter-loop routing switch. If TRN can selectively gate sensory thalamocortical pathways, the same architecture could gate between sensory (E1), motor (E2), and cognitive (E3) thalamocortical loops.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study demonstrates routing between sensory modalities (visual vs auditory), not between processing hierarchy levels (sensory vs motor vs deliberative). Extrapolation from modality-gating to loop-gating is architecturally plausible but not directly tested.
mapping.source_context: Mouse, optogenetic manipulation + electrophysiology, cross-modal attention task.

confidence_components.notes: Exceptional source quality (Nature, causal optogenetic evidence, clean behavioral readout). Mapping fidelity moderate: TRN subnetworks are modality-specific, but whether they also gate between functional hierarchy levels (sensory/motor/cognitive) is inferred, not tested. Transfer risk: mouse model, though TRN architecture is conserved across mammals.
